content:
⸻

Prompt 6 – Medical Credibility and Improper Substitution of Judgment
Analyze case law prohibiting employers from substituting their judgment for that of qualified medical providers. Apply this to the employer’s insistence on additional records and apparent skepticism toward the treating provider, despite an IMC’s confirmation of ADA-qualifying disabilities. Address how such conduct may support both failure-to-accommodate and retaliation claims.

⸻

Prompt 7 – Comparator and Policy Evidence: Remote Work Practices
Investigate how courts evaluate comparator evidence and informal practices when employers claim “no remote work policy.” Analyze pandemic-era telework, adjunct remote teaching norms, and discretionary offsite work allowances as evidence undermining the employer’s asserted inflexibility. Frame discovery inferences where the employer controls access to comparator data.

⸻

Prompt 8 – Damages Causation and Foreseeability
Develop a damages analysis linking ADA violations to economic and non-economic losses. Address back pay, front pay, lost adjunct income, medical expenses, relocation losses, and career disruption. Apply foreseeability principles and ADA remedial standards to show that cascading harms were a natural and proximate result of the employer’s conduct, relying on the detailed damages summary .
